title: From Corporate Law to Fighting Human Trafficking: One Woman's Bold Leadership
guestBio: Leigh Dundas is a human rights attorney, modern-day abolitionist, and award-winning public speaker who transitioned from representing Fortune 500 companies to leading undercover missions against child trafficking networks. She's become a powerful voice for civil liberties and medical freedom, advocating for human rights both globally and domestically.
excerpt: Human rights attorney Leigh Dundas shares her extraordinary journey from high-stakes corporate litigation to leading dangerous undercover missions against child trafficking. This conversation reveals what true courage looks like in leadership.
metaDescription: Human rights attorney Leigh Dundas on transitioning from corporate law to fighting trafficking and defending civil liberties with bold leadership.
keyTakeaways: True leadership often requires making bold career pivots that align with your deeper values and purpose.
Protecting human rights and freedoms requires both grassroots action and individual courage to stand up for what's right.
The emotional toll of advocacy work demands intentional self-care strategies and strong support systems.
Corporate skills can be powerfully redirected toward social justice and humanitarian causes.
Medical freedom and civil liberties are fundamental human rights worth defending at every level.
Undercover operations against trafficking require meticulous planning, courage, and collaboration with local authorities.
Grassroots movements have the power to create meaningful change when individuals step up to lead.

pullQuotes.text: Leadership isn't about titles or corner offices—it's about having the courage to fight for what's right, even when the stakes are high.

timestamps.label: Leigh's transition from corporate law to human rights
Fighting for medical freedom and civil liberties
Managing the emotional toll of advocacy work

content:
What does it take to walk away from a successful corporate law career to fight some of the world's most dangerous battles? In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, Aimee sits down with Leigh Dundas, a woman who redefined what leadership looks like when she traded Fortune 500 boardrooms for undercover operations against child trafficking networks.

Leigh's story isn't just about career transformation—it's about the kind of courage that changes the world. As a human rights attorney and modern-day abolitionist, she's proven that sometimes the most important leadership happens far from the spotlight, in the dangerous places where justice hangs in the balance.

The Bold Career Pivot That Changed Everything

You know that feeling when your current success feels hollow compared to your deeper calling? Leigh experienced this tension while representing major corporations in high-stakes litigation. Despite her professional achievements, she felt pulled toward something more meaningful—defending human rights and fighting for those who couldn't fight for themselves.

This wasn't a gradual shift or a side project that slowly grew. Leigh made a deliberate decision to use her legal expertise in service of human dignity, even when it meant leaving behind financial security and professional prestige. Her journey reminds us that true leadership often requires us to risk what we have for what we believe in.

The transition from corporate law to human rights advocacy required Leigh to develop entirely new skills and networks. She had to learn how to work with international organizations, understand different legal systems, and navigate the complex world of humanitarian work. Most importantly, she had to develop the emotional resilience required for work that exposes you to humanity's darkest corners.

Behind the Scenes of Anti-Trafficking Operations

When Leigh describes her undercover work against child trafficking networks in Southeast Asia, you realize how far true leadership can take you from your comfort zone. These weren't theoretical legal battles fought in conference rooms—they were dangerous, real-world operations requiring meticulous planning, split-second decision-making, and incredible personal courage.

The work demanded collaboration with local authorities, international organizations, and other advocates who shared her commitment to protecting vulnerable children. Leigh had to learn to navigate different cultures, legal systems, and operational challenges while maintaining the secrecy essential for successful interventions.

What strikes you most about Leigh's approach is how she applied her corporate training to humanitarian work. The same attention to detail, strategic thinking, and risk assessment that served her in business litigation became essential tools in planning operations that could literally save lives. Her story shows how professional skills can be powerfully redirected toward causes that matter.

Fighting for Freedom at Home

Leigh's advocacy work isn't limited to international operations. She's become a powerful voice for medical freedom and civil liberties here in the United States, standing up for fundamental human rights that many people take for granted. This domestic advocacy requires different skills but the same unwavering commitment to justice.

Fighting for civil liberties at home means challenging systems and institutions that may be more familiar but are equally entrenched. Leigh has learned to navigate media attention, political pressure, and public scrutiny while maintaining focus on the core principles that drive her work. She understands that protecting freedom requires vigilance and courage at every level of society.

Her work demonstrates that human rights advocacy isn't just about dramatic international rescues—it's also about the daily work of protecting freedoms that form the foundation of a just society. This kind of leadership requires you to speak truth to power, even when it's uncomfortable or unpopular.

Managing the Emotional Weight of Justice Work

One of the most honest parts of this conversation is when Leigh discusses the emotional toll of her work. When you're fighting human trafficking, defending civil liberties, and confronting injustice at its worst, the psychological impact can be overwhelming. Leigh has had to develop strategies for processing trauma, maintaining hope, and preventing burnout.

She's learned that sustainable advocacy requires intentional self-care, strong support systems, and clear boundaries between work and personal life. The people fighting for justice need to protect their own wellbeing so they can continue showing up for others. This isn't selfish—it's strategic.

Leigh's approach to managing emotional stress includes connecting with other advocates who understand the work, maintaining spiritual practices that ground her, and remembering that every small victory contributes to larger change. She's learned to celebrate progress while staying realistic about the magnitude of the challenges ahead.

What Real Leadership Looks Like

Through Leigh's story, you see leadership stripped of all pretense and reduced to its essence—the willingness to act on your convictions regardless of personal cost. Her work requires making decisions under pressure, inspiring others to take risks for important causes, and maintaining hope when facing seemingly insurmountable challenges.

This kind of leadership isn't about charisma or public speaking skills—it's about the quiet courage to do what's right when no one is watching. It's about using whatever skills and resources you have in service of something bigger than yourself. Leigh proves that leadership can happen anywhere when you decide to take responsibility for creating the change you want to see.

Her journey challenges every excuse we make for not stepping up, not speaking out, or not taking action on issues we care about. If one person can transition from corporate law to fighting international trafficking rings, what's stopping the rest of us from leading in our own spheres of influence?

Leigh Dundas reminds us that the most important battles for justice happen when ordinary people decide to do extraordinary things. Her story isn't just inspiring—it's a call to action for anyone ready to transform their skills, passion, and courage into leadership that changes the world.

guestLinks.label: 📚 Grab your copy of The Manage Her

quiz.title: What's Your Values-Driven Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Ever wondered how you'd step up when your deepest values are on the line? This quiz reveals how you naturally lead when what matters most is at stake.

questions.question: When you discover your current role conflicts with your deeper values, what's your typical response?

options.text: Start planning an exit strategy — life's too short for work that doesn't matter
Look for ways to gradually shift your responsibilities toward more meaningful projects
Find small ways to honor your values within your current situation while supporting others
Speak up about the conflict and push for changes, even if it creates tension

questions.question: What draws you most to leadership opportunities?

options.text: The chance to create something completely new and transformative
The opportunity to build sustainable systems that create lasting impact
The ability to support and protect people who need an advocate
The platform to defend important principles and stand up for what's right

questions.question: When facing a major life decision, you tend to:

options.text: Trust your gut and make the leap, even if others think you're crazy
Carefully research all options and create a detailed plan before moving forward
Consider how your choice will affect the people you care about most
Ask yourself what choice you could live with and what would compromise your integrity

questions.question: Your ideal way to create change in the world would be:

options.text: Making a dramatic career shift that becomes an inspiring example for others
Developing programs or organizations that address root causes systematically
Mentoring and supporting individuals to help them reach their potential
Fighting against injustices and defending the rights of those who can't defend themselves

questions.question: What kind of courage do you most admire in others?

options.text: The willingness to completely reinvent yourself for something you believe in
The patience to work steadily toward long-term goals despite setbacks
The strength to keep caring and supporting others even when it's emotionally difficult
The backbone to stand up to powerful people or systems when principles are at stake

questions.question: When you imagine your legacy, what matters most to you?

options.text: That you showed others it's possible to completely change course and follow your calling
That you built something meaningful that continues to help people long after you're gone
That the people in your life felt truly seen, supported, and valued
That you never compromised on your core values, even when it cost you

results.title: The Bold Pivot
results.description: You're wired to make dramatic changes when your values call for it. Like Leigh leaving corporate law, you're not afraid to walk away from success if it doesn't align with your deeper purpose. Your superpower is the courage to completely reinvent yourself for a cause bigger than personal gain. Leigh's story will inspire you to trust that bold leap you've been considering.

results.title: The Strategic Builder
results.description: You create lasting change through careful planning and systematic action. You understand that meaningful impact requires both vision and execution, and you're willing to do the unglamorous work of building sustainable movements. Your strength lies in translating big ideas into actionable strategies. Leigh's methodical approach to fighting trafficking will give you a masterclass in turning passion into powerful results.

results.title: The Quiet Guardian
results.description: Your leadership style is about protecting and nurturing what matters most, often behind the scenes. You lead through deep relationships and steady support, creating safe spaces for others to thrive. Your power is in the consistency of your care and the strength of your convictions. Leigh's emphasis on self-care and support systems will resonate deeply with your protective instincts.

results.title: The Boundary Warrior
results.description: You lead by standing firm on principles, even when it's uncomfortable or costly. Like Leigh defending civil liberties, you're not afraid to be the person who says 'no' when lines are crossed. Your courage shows up in defending others and holding the line on what's right. This episode will validate your instinct to fight for fundamental freedoms and human dignity.

transcript:
Hey everyone, welcome back to The Manage Her, the podcast where we illuminate the often unseen work of women, transforming it into a narrative of leadership, resilience, and empowerment. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author, entrepreneur, and mama of six.

Today, we're joined by a formidable advocate for freedom and justice, Leigh Dundas. Leigh is a renowned human rights attorney, modern-day abolitionist, and award-winning public speaker. With a career spanning over three decades, she began representing Fortune 500 companies in high-stakes litigation and later transitioned to humanitarian work, focusing on combating child slavery and defending civil liberties.

Her journey has taken her from corporate boardrooms to the front lines of global advocacy, including undercover operations in Southeast Asia to dismantle child trafficking networks and challenging medical overreach in the United States. In this episode, we'll delve into the pivotal moments that shaped her transition from corporate law to human rights advocacy, her courageous efforts in combating global injustices, and the personal convictions that fuel her relentless pursuit of justice. Let's jump in.

**Aimee:** Hi, how are you? Thank you so much for being here. First of all, you're amazing. Big fan.

**Leigh:** Well, thank you. I was listening to your windup and I'm like, man, AI makes me sound really good. Nobody would know that I was licking mascara off my finger in my car at a stoplight just now.

**Aimee:** Weren't we all doing the mom thing at the stoplight? Like, oh, my mascara leaks all over my purse and now I'm a mechanic just in time for a podcast interview. So, anyway, thank you for having me. It's awesome to be here.

**Aimee:** Yeah. So, so happy to have you. What inspired your shift from representing Fortune 500 companies to focusing on human rights and combating child slavery?

**Leigh:** Yeah. Well, actually it shifted a little before I jumped right into the child slavery. So, I became a mom, to your point of your whole podcast. And people don't know this a lot, but when you work at big law firms representing Fortune 500 companies, you basically work every hour that you're not actually asleep. And even when you're actually sleeping, sometimes you're actually still working, frighteningly enough.

So, I decided I didn't want my child to think that I was like some passing random aunt when I came home because that's all the minutes of the day I was spending with them. You think it's a joke, but we only had one female partner in my law firm in Newport Beach's office when I got married and she came to work all upset one day because her baby had called the nanny mom.

**Aimee:** And I was like, "Girl, you've been representing Western Digital like 3,000 hours this year. Does the kid even know you exist? You can't really fault the nanny and the kid for getting it confused over there."

**Leigh:** And that's fine for a lot of people, but I didn't – I'd already killed a lot of house plants and almost a cat. I thought I got to get it right with the kid. I really need to focus because I am not preschool teacher endowed talent-wise. So, I started working with my husband. Andy had a small chiropractic office at the time and it was just much more flexible and that's what I was doing before I gave birth and then I kept doing that right up until we sold his office about 12 years ago now.

And we thought, yay, we have a little bit of money and a little bit of time. Let's go travel the world for a few months. So, we ripped my daughter who was age eight in third grade out of school. Best thing we ever did. I highly encourage every parent listening to do it before the kid is 16 and go travel before they get lives of their own because it's much easier when they're like portable and just sort of hip attached.

And we got down to Australia and we were going to spend four to six months traveling and I had never not worked. So within three days of being in Australia enjoying the good life of semi-retired not working I was bored off my ass and I'd always wanted to do human rights work. I'd been very interested in studying and representing and working and volunteering in vulnerable communities – rape survivor, child abuse, domestic violence type stuff.

And I thought, well, I'm just going to cold call some anti-sex slavery nonprofits down here in Southeast Asia, Australasia, and surely they need an extra set of hands. And it didn't go the way I thought. I was cold calling out of the yellow pages and they'd pick up the phone and they'd be like, "Let me get this straight. You're a white do-gooder mummy from LA living down in Australia and you're bored and want to help?" Yeah. Bye-bye.

**Aimee:** Really?

**Leigh:** Oh, yeah. Oh, wow. And then finally, I don't know, a month later, we were in New Zealand. By the time I got a call back, finally, somebody bit, and it was, they were right down here in Newport Beach. It was a guy who had opened Vietnamese orphanages. He and his wife did after adopting two orphans and not being able to adopt them all. And they were seeing a lot of trafficking through their orphanages and orphan systems in Vietnam.

And they had invited friends of theirs from I think it was either Saddleback or Mariners Church down here who had started an anti-slavery nonprofit which was called A21 campaign. It stands for abolishing 21st century slavery. That had been opened by two Christian pastors, Nick and Christine Caine. And so that whole executive crew and the Vietnamese orphan owner were all flying to Da Nang Vietnam on May 23rd of 2013. And sight unseen off a voicemail I'd left a month earlier, they invited me to the party in Da Nang.

And I told my husband and daughter, I think we're going to blow this popsicle stand. We left New Zealand, headed into Vietnam. I went to some meetings with them, fell in love with the work, came home with a job.

**Aimee:** Wow. Oh my gosh. So, tell me a little bit about that work.

**Leigh:** Well, I mean, nominally, I'd been hired as their GC.

**Aimee:** General counsel.

**Leigh:** Yeah. Which stands for general counsel. It's basically a desk jockey kind of thing where you're looking at contracts and they would have a walk for freedom, like a kind of breast cancer awareness walk every year and like, oh, we need a contract in case somebody trips and breaks their ankle. Well, that kind of thing. So, I was doing a lot of that kind of work, but they had started rescuing girls in Europe, in Greece, and they were starting to open a Southeast Asia office.

So technically I was their in-house lawyer, but in actuality I was almost living really part-time in Thailand and flying back and forth. Two weeks here, two weeks there. My daughter was still in third grade. And opening up an office down there. And we were moving away from just doing the rescue work and into the brothel busting work. So we'd hired a crew of former SWAT Navy Seal type special forces type skill set type guys.

And one of my very first jobs, I'll never forget Amy, it was so funny. They're like, "Well, we're going to need to outfit them." And I'm like, "Yeah, yeah." And I'm like, "Do you guys have any of the tech?" And they're like, "Yeah, no, just go research it and get some." So, there I am on my laptop looking up like 007 gadgets. And they make all this really cool stuff like pens and hats and little pocket protectors that are actually low-light high def audio video recording devices which you need in bars and brothels.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Leigh:** And we ordered a bunch of this stuff from Europe and outfitted our guys with it and sent them off into the brothels and they started developing intel on how many girls are in the brothel. Where do they hail from? 90% – people don't know this but 90% of the girls that we were working with and trying to rescue in the Thailand region actually come from Laos.

**Aimee:** Laos.

**Leigh:** Laos, which is the southern province that borders Thailand and people are like well why do 90% of the girls in brothels come from Laos and I'm like because Laos is a currently communist country and a lot of the other countries neighboring Cambodia of course had a communist revolution when you and I were kids in the 70s, late 70s where Pol Pot came in and was going to make everybody equal and equally happy. Sounds like...

**Aimee:** Khmer Rouge, right?

**Leigh:** Khmer Rouge came in and wiped out a quarter of their population. Shot them face down into shallow graves.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Well, and only the educated people, right?

**Leigh:** Oh, yeah. If you didn't have calluses and you had glasses, i.e. me. Although this work week I've been cleaning my garage, so I'm starting to get some calluses. But yeah, I would have been first up to bat for being hatcheted in the back and left to fall in a shallow grave because they wanted to wipe out the intellectual class.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Leigh:** Because we're the ones who can actually push back against an overthrow.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Leigh:** We've got resources. We've got brain power. We've got skill sets to bring and typically we can see it coming because we've been educated enough to sort of see the writing on the wall perhaps before some of the other folks. And so the communists were very smart. They didn't want push back. So they got rid of the people that could push back.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Leigh:** They walked in on a glorious spring day like this in the late 70s and sent the Khmer Rouge clad in black pajama pants, young guys with hatchets, to round up all of the school teachers and architects and accountants and lawyers and rich people and anybody who had a profession. And they lied.

They said, "Oh, it's just going to be for a little while. Just take the clothes on your back and you're going to go out into the farmland and you're going to learn how to be farmers and we're all going to work for the communal good and you're going to..." I mean these people didn't know how to farm.

**Aimee:** Sure.

**Leigh:** And they weren't actually ever going to become farmers. They were eating crickets to stay alive and given a half cup of rice. And yeah, it was Auschwitz all over again in the 70s, but this time under the far-left regime as opposed to the far right. It wasn't fascist, it was communist. But six of one half a dozen of the other once you have that kind of autocracy and dictatorship.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Leigh:** And we had been – when I met A21, my daughter and husband were with me. We were traveling together and we left Vietnam and we went to Cambodia. So before I started doing work down there, my daughter and husband and I went to the Killing Fields.

Not intentionally. We'd been on a tour and the tour guide the day before had said, "You should really go see the Killing Fields." And I'm like, "Isn't that where like most of your Cambodian population was executed when I was a kid?" And I have a little girl.

He's like, "Yeah, yeah, it's just a bunch of empty fields. There's nothing scary."

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Leigh:** And you can't get uncensored internet down there. So I did try to do my due diligence. I'm like, "Killing fields." And I'm like, "Oh, yeah. It just looks like some empty fields like you'd see down here in OC."

**Aimee:** Right.

**Leigh:** Out by the coast. And off we went. And it was a self-guided tour. And my daughter begged to hear the tour and we said no. And she said, "I'm a human and that's not fair and this should be educational." And I'm like, "Oh." And I said, "Okay, compromise. Daddy and I listen to the tracks and if it's not too scary, you can stay a track behind us on this two-mile walkabout tour."

What we didn't realize because we couldn't get uncensored internet down there is that at the end of the tour they have a four-story tall square box monument that's all glass on all the walls and it's about the size of this room and every year when the rains come to this day they will wash away the top soil and the bones of the dead from the late 70s...

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Leigh:** ...will rise to the top and so this never happens again in Cambodia, those who have survived, the descendants, very carefully and gingerly gather up the bones and put them in the little monument categorized by long bones, skulls, you name it, and with a little sign in Cambodian that says something along the lines of, "So we never forget."

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Leigh:** So my daughter spies at the very end of this tour this big building that we can't really clearly see. And like any self-respecting 8-year-old, she tears off over to it. And by the time fat little mommy and daddy catch up, she is eyeball to eye socket with a skull of a child that had been executed.

**Aimee:** Oh wow.

**Leigh:** Probably at about the age she was. And she looked at me and she said, "Mama, how did this happen?" And I knelt down and I yanked her little jaw away from the glass case and I made her make eye contact with me because at that point, in for a penny, in for a pound. And I said, "Those in power lied. And by the time the good people figured it out, it was too damn late. Let this be a lesson to you, my love. Don't you ever take the word of somebody in a position of authority simply because they're in a position of power. If anything, as a baseline, you distrust them until they've proven themselves."

And my husband and I were taking bets on the way home as to how much therapy we were going to have to pay for.

**Aimee:** I love this. I read about this in your book and I absolutely loved it.

**Leigh:** Not a single session of therapy. And she's about to graduate college and I will tell you she's in the middle of liberal hell. She's at a California college, a well-known one where our lovely senator goes to offer free extra credit to all the med school students on that campus to come push through the bills in Sacramento. And she's got my last name. And when they had little tents out in favor of Palestine a year ago and all her little friends were like rah rah and didn't even know what they were rahing about. My daughter's like, you need to check yourself. Do you understand history? Do you understand Auschwitz? Do you understand fascism? Do you understand communism? Do you really understand what's going on here? Let me tell you something. You want your children to be raised in a way that is absolutely teflon against all the crazy that's going on right now? Knock off the Grand Caymans in the Grand Canyon. Take your kid to Cambodia this summer.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Leigh:** It's a tremendous lesson in where you can end up if you don't stand up. When people don't stand up.

**Aimee:** When people don't stand up. And that's why I named my book Just Stand Up.

**Leigh:** Just stand up.

**Aimee:** Yeah. We got to stand up. We got to do a better job if we don't want to repeat things like that.

**Leigh:** Yeah. Absolutely. And we have started going down a real slippery slope here and it was under the guise of safety.

**Aimee:** Safety.

**Leigh:** Safety and the greater good.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Leigh:** Yeah. It was the same playbook that Hitler used. I mean, it was all about keeping the Germans safe from typhus. The Jews were carriers of typhus, don't you know? Of course, everybody's a carrier of typhus when you put them in Auschwitz and Warsaw ghettos because they're living cheek by jowl and there's no sanitation and fleas carry disease. But at the beginning, they weren't carriers of anything anymore than you and I were. But there's always some pretext. There's always some false flag and our children need to understand so that they can actually not go running like lemmings and sheep over a cliff.

**Aimee:** Right.

**Leigh:** But yeah, that's how I got started doing the anti-slavery work. And people are like, "Well, how the heck do you get so poor you need to sell your eldest daughter to a brothel?" And I'm like, "You live in a communist country."

**Aimee:** Oh.

**Leigh:** Footnote for why you need to stand up here in America if you don't want this to turn communist on you before the next election.

**Aimee:** Exactly. Yeah. Yeah. You're right. Yeah. So these poor communists sell their daughters.

**Leigh:** Yeah. And I didn't – to be fair because you walk into doing this or I'll keep it to my judging a book by its cover. Prejudging. We all do. I mean, human beings stereotype. If we didn't, we wouldn't be able to get through the day with the decisions that would overwhelm us. So, we stereotype and it's a safe thing and it's a biological imperative or our brain would just be overwhelmed. That's the sociological psych underside of stereotyping.

Of course, it's not cool to stereotype, especially if you're doing it along race or sex or national origin lines, but you need to be able to put stars and shapes and triangles over here and stars over there. And we do it in our kitchen and we do it driving down the street. And we do it in a lot of places.

But with that said, and by way of excusing my own prejudging, I walked in to the brothel network assuming that there were good guys and bad guys.

**Aimee:** Sure.

**Leigh:** And then we took a crew just like the one you have here, just about the same size. We had a couple tripods. It was a Christian film crew out of Australia, and we took them into the brothel town I worked in. It has 140 child brothels in it. It's at the very bottom of Thailand that buttresses Malaysia. And there's a river that divides the countries. And on the Thailand side, it's Buddhist and on the Malay side in that region, not all of Malaysia, but in that region, it is radical jihadi controlled.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Leigh:** So, all of the terror training cells that teach people how to bomb the USS Cole or other targets, they're there in South Asia and they practice just like you and I practice piano or public speaking or keeping our house clean. They practice their bombing skills. And the brothels over the river are the perfect fodder to bomb.

So, the very first year I worked there, we had 330 bombs go off.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Leigh:** Yeah. Which is like a bomb a day. And we were there and we wanted to get the word out on how Asian trafficking situations were a little different than Europe. So we had brought this film crew into the brothel, one of the brothels and only one of the girls had been courageous enough to volunteer to be interviewed and we did the whole low-light shadow 60 minutes kind of setup. So you're just seeing shadowy profile.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Leigh:** And she was maybe 12 or 13 years old and it was this time of day. It was twilight heading into the night and she's like, "Well, I only have a little bit of time. I don't want to be late for my first date."

And we popped her in the corner and we had our tripods on her and we asked her, "Did you know when you got to this brothel town, because she was from Laos originally, not Thailand. Did you know what you would be doing when you got here?"

And I'll never forget her answer. She looked dead into the camera and she said, "I knew exactly what I would be doing when I got here, but I'm not unhappy." She was speaking in her native tongue. We were having translators. So, there's a pause and the English translation of that comes back to us and my British colleague who's sitting over here leans over and he goes, "Leigh, nobody is happy to be in a brothel. Let's ask it again. There's something wrong with the translation."

So we asked it a second time, did you know when you got here what you would be doing? And for the second time in as many minutes, she looked dead into the camera and she said, "I knew exactly what I'd be doing and I'm not unhappy." And then she explained because my seven brothers and sisters have food on the table and they're not starving to death, nor is my mother.

**Aimee:** And you could have heard a pin drop in the room at that point. I looked over to the corner. I've got two guys squatting down who are 20 years old, hands on their cameras. They got tears rolling off their chin. I had tears rolling off my chin. And I was like, "Well, don't I need to check my privilege at the door?"

Because I had walked into this room in a brothel, assuming that any family who sold their child to a brothel was the devil. How could you sell your child to a brothel? And what I learned is that is a very privileged thought because there but for the grace of God go you and I. You have no idea what you would do if you were so flipping freaking poor that 100% of your six children were going to starve to death this year if you didn't sell one along with you and your husband.

**Aimee:** Right.

**Leigh:** So if you and your husband and all of them are for sure going to die. Talk about a Hobson's choice. Talk about a catch-22.

**Aimee:** Right.

**Leigh:** Do you sell one to save the rest? Now they're all alive, including the one you sold for $300 US so that you can feed yourself. That's what the going rate was. $300 US gets a family of seven in Laos, nine, 10 people in Laos through a year. It's brutal. We see the world through a very privileged lens. We really do. I talk about the privileged women of the West. And in some ways, our privilege is why we have to get off the bench. It's why we have to stand up because we can.

**Aimee:** Because we can.

**Leigh:** I don't want to be living in a communist country or this might be your story one day. And I'm not excusing parents who sell their children. I am just saying until you walk a mile in another person's shoes, not to rehash that cliche, but it's a very different situation than I had thought walking into it. I thought anybody who sells their kid is evil. And what I realized is they're so freaking poor that that's just a different option on the buffet table than you and I have ever had to face.

**Aimee:** Right.

**Leigh:** And but you need to not have a communist government. You need to not have a fascist government. You need to not have a dictatorial government. And we're going in the right direction right now and we need to keep it that way.
